64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. 72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84

86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.

Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.

A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. 202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.

204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300	
301	## Logging
302	
303	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
304	
305	```markdown
306	# Blueprint Writer Report
307	
308	## Slug
309	<slug>
310	
311	## Status
312	<COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
313	<If INCOMPLETE: which required items could not be written and why.>
314	
315	## Target chapter
316	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
317	
318	## Changes Made
319	- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:foo}`/`\lean{Foo.bar}` — <one line on what it captures>
320	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
321	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
322	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
323	- **Fixed dependencies** `<label>` — corrected/added `\uses{...}` (was isolated / had a broken ref per leandag).
324	- **Removed** `<label>` — orphaned isolated block, removal authorized by directive.
325	- ...
326	
327	## Cross-references introduced
328	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
329	- ...
330	
331	## References consulted
332	<every local file under `references/` you OPENED and READ this session, with a one-line note on what you took from each. The plan agent and the reviewer use this list to verify that every `% SOURCE:` parenthetical points at a file you actually read. Omit this section only when you wrote zero citation blocks (Archon-original chapter).>
333	- `references/hartshorne-III-5.md` — verbatim quote for `\thm:smooth_criterion` (statement + proof).
334	- `references/stacks-tag-01V4.md` — verbatim quote for `\def:etale_morphism`.
335	
336	## Macros needed (if any)
337	- `\foo{...}` — used in <where>; needs definition in `macros/common.tex`. NOT added by me (out of write-domain).
338	
339	## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
340	- slug `<child-slug>`: requested `<source>`. Status: COMPLETE / NOT_FOUND / PARTIAL. Summary at `references/<slug>.md`.
341	- ...
342	
343	## Notes for Plan Agent
344	- <any inconsistency I noticed in sibling chapters that the directive didn't cover>
345	- <any structural concern: e.g. "the chapter is now 800 lines, consider splitting">
346	- <any reference I had to guess at because the directive's reference section was incomplete>
347	
348	## Strategy-modifying findings
349	<populate this section ONLY if writing the chapter surfaced a need to
350	change the project's strategy itself — not just the chapter content.
351	Examples:
352	- A definition you were asked to write turns out to require a property
353	  that the strategy assumes is automatic but is not.
354	- A theorem you sketched is provable as stated, but its consequence
355	  used elsewhere in STRATEGY.md does not follow from it.
356	- A reference cited as authoritative actually requires a different
357	  setup than the strategy assumes.

359	When this section is non-empty, the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md

323	/-- **Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push–pull head.**
324	For composable scheme morphisms `f : A ⟶ B`, `p : B ⟶ Z` and `N : Z.Modules`, the

332	lemma pushPull_unit_mate {A B Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : A ⟶ B) (p : B ⟶ Z)
333	    (N : Z.Modules) :
334	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).unit.app N ≫
335	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p).map
336	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).unit.app
337	            ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) ≫
338	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp f p).hom.app
339	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) =
340	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p)).unit.app N ≫
341	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (f ≫ p)).map
342	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv.app N) := by
343	  have key := unit_conjugateEquiv
344	    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).comp
345	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f))
346	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p))
347	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv N
348	  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv, Adjunction.comp_unit_app] at key
349	  simpa only [Category.assoc] using key
350	
351	/-- **Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push–pull tail** (kernel-cheap
352	generalised form). The morphism map `pushPullMap` glues its pullback-comparison leg
353	to the target object `pushPullObj F Y₂` by two `eqToHom` coercions along the
354	over-triangle `g.left ≫ Y₁.hom = Y₂.hom`. Cancelling those coercions *in situ*
355	(at the concrete pushforward/pullback objects) provokes a kernel `whnf` blow-up.
356	This lemma states the cancellation **with the over-triangle equality as a free

359	`simp`. Applying it to `pushPullMap` via `rw` rewrites the tail without forcing the

361	`eqToHom ≫ (pushforward p₂).map (pullbackComp).hom ≫ eqToHom` collapses to the
362	transport-light `(pushforward (gl ≫ p₁)).map (pullbackComp).hom ≫ eqToHom`, the
363	single residual `eqToHom` carrying the unavoidable object identification of the
364	codomain `pushPullObj F Y₂`. Reusable pre-coherence brick for `pushPullMap_comp`. -/
365	lemma pushPull_transport_cancel {Y₁ Y₂ : Scheme.{u}}
366	    (gl : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (p₁ : Y₁ ⟶ X) (p₂ : Y₂ ⟶ X)
367	    (h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂) (F : X.Modules) :
368	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
369	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback gl).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F))) h) ≫
370	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
371	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).obj
372	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) h) =
373	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (gl ≫ p₁)).map
374	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
375	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
376	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) h) := by
377	  subst h
378	  simp

382	`O_X`-modules, produce the relative {\v C}ech cochain complex in `QCoh(S)` by:
383	forgetting the augmentation (`CosimplicialObject.Augmented.drop`), pushing the

393	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f)).obj (CosimplicialObject.Augmented.drop.obj N))

396	Schemes, `lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial`).

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application},
10	% \texttt{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}) (read from
11	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
16	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
17	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
18	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
19	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
20	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
21	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
22	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
23	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
24	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
25	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
26	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
27	
28	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
29	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
30	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
31	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
32	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
33	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
34	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
35	\[
36	  \begin{array}{ccc}
37	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
38	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
39	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
40	  \end{array}
41	\]
42	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
43	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
44	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
45	
46	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
47	
48	\begin{definition}
49	
50	\leanok
51	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
52	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
53	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
54	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, lem:push_pull_functor}
55	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
56	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
57	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
58	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
59	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
60	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
61	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
62	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
63	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
64	  % the unit ideal, see
65	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
66	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
67	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
68	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
69	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
70	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
71	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
72	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
73	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
74	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
75	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
76	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
77	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
78	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
79	  \[
80	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
81	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
82	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
83	  \]
84	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
85	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
86	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
87	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
88	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
89	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
90	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
91	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
92	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
93	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
94	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
95	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
96	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
97	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).

164	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
165	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
166	
167	It is convenient to factor the construction of
168	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
169	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the entire mathematical
170	content that is not yet available off the shelf is isolated in the middle piece.
171	
172	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
173	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
174	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
175	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
176	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
177	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
178	\[
179	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
180	    \;=\;
181	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
182	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
183	\]
184	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
185	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
186	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
187	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
188	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
189	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
190	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
191	
192	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
193	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
194	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
195	\[
196	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
197	  \qquad
198	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
199	\]
200	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
201	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
202	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
203	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
204	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
205	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
206	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
207	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
208	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
209	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
210	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
211	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
212	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
213	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
214	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
215	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. This
216	functoriality is \emph{not} coupled to the project's tensor--pullback substrate;
217	it consumes only Mathlib's pseudofunctor packaging of pullback and pushforward.
218	Concretely, pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
219	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
220	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
221	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
222	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
223	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts about this
224	packaging: the mate identity expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback
225	comparison as the pushforward comparison (the lemma
226	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}), its identity normalisation
227	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom}, and the pseudofunctor unitor and pentagon
228	(associativity) coherences \texttt{pseudofunctor\_left\_unitality},
229	\texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality}, \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity}.
230	All of these are already available off the shelf, so the functoriality of the
231	relative-direct-image functor is a \emph{consumer of Mathlib's coherence},
232	independent of the project-local sheafification/pullback machinery of the
233	tensor--pullback substrate. The laws are nonetheless not formal trivialities: each
234	is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison isomorphisms along
235	the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and past the adjunction
236	unit, gluing them across two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions; the obstacle is the
237	bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
238	
239	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
240	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
241	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
242	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
243	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
244	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
245	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
246	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
247	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
248	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
249	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
250	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
251	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
252	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
253	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
254	that content is the pushforward/pullback pseudofunctor coherence supplied by
255	Mathlib (the comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
256	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
257	and their unitor/pentagon identities), not any project-local ingredient.
258	
259	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
260	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
261	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
262	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
263	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
264	
265	\begin{definition}\leanok
266	[Arrow of an affine cover]
267	  \label{def:cover_arrow}

270	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
271	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
272	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
273	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
274	  apply.
275	\end{definition}

289	  \]

307	\begin{definition}\leanok
308	[Push--pull comparison map]
309	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap}
311	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
312	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
313	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
314	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
315	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
316	  \[
317	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
318	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
319	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
320	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
321	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
322	      \;=\;
323	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
324	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
325	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
326	  \]
327	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
328	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
329	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
330	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
331	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
332	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
333	\end{definition}

336	[Push--pull functor assembly]
337	  \label{lem:push_pull_functor}
338	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp}
340	  % NOTE (iter-264): only `pushPullMap_id` exists in Lean (landed axiom-clean this iter);

345	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, def:cech_nerve}
346	  The object and morphism assignments of
347	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} assemble into a
348	  functor \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\): the
349	  comparison maps respect identities and composition,
350	  \[
351	    G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)},
352	    \qquad
353	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g),
354	  \]
355	  the latter for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of
356	  \(X\)-schemes. Composing \(G\) with the backbone of
357	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
358	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.

361	\begin{proof}
362	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
363	  Both laws are statements purely about the pushforward/pullback comparison
364	  isomorphisms and carry no sectionwise content. The route is the adjunction-mate
365	  calculus applied to the head \(p_{1*}(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward
366	  comparison in the composite defining \(G(g)\): rewriting this head as the
367	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison
368	  \((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) — via \texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv} —
369	  turns both laws into identities among the pseudofunctor's structure \(2\)-cells.
370	
371	  For \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}\): after the mate rewrite the identity leg
372	  collapses by the identity normalisation \texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom}
373	  together with the right unitality \texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality};
374	  the two over-triangle coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
375	
376	  For \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\): the same mate rewrite reduces the claim to
377	  reassociating the three pullback comparisons, which is the pseudofunctor pentagon
378	  \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity}; the inner adjunction unit is slid past the
379	  outer pushforward by naturality of the unit (\(\mathrm{Adjunction.unit\_naturality}\)).
380	
381	  The content is entirely Mathlib-side pseudofunctor coherence; the work is the
382	  bookkeeping of the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle
383	  \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\). No step invokes the project-local sheafification/pullback
384	  square, so the laws are independent of the tensor--pullback substrate.
385	\end{proof}

508	  vanishing for quasi-coherent sheaves on affine schemes.)}

667	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
668	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}; unconditional
669	  {\v C}ech packaging is Archon-original.}
670	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
671	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Fix a finite affine open cover
672	  \(\mathfrak{U}\) of \(X\). The \emph{(unconditional) \(i\)-th higher direct
673	  image} is defined, for each \(i \geq 0\), as the \(i\)-th cohomology sheaf of the
674	  relative {\v C}ech complex,
675	  \[
676	    R^i f_*\mathcal{F} \;:=\;
677	      H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
678	      \in \operatorname{QCoh}(S).
679	  \]
680	  This requires \emph{no} hypothesis that the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
681	  have enough injectives: the right-hand side is the cohomology of an explicit
682	  complex of quasi-coherent sheaves. By
683	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} the result is canonically isomorphic to
684	  the derived-functor higher direct image wherever the latter is defined, and in
685	  particular is independent of the chosen affine cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) up to
686	  canonical isomorphism: two affine covers admit a common affine refinement, and
687	  the induced refinement maps on {\v C}ech complexes are quasi-isomorphisms because
688	  both compute the same \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) by
689	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}. For \(i = 0\) one recovers the ordinary
690	  pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
691	\end{definition}
